content: Refactor `src/api/client.ts` to handle retries and surface API errors as a typed exception. Keep the public exports stable.

I will start by reading the current `client.ts` to understand the structure, then introduce a typed `ApiError` and a small retry wrapper.

Before I touch any code, a few things I want to verify up front:

1. **Public surface area.** The user explicitly asked to keep the public exports stable. Anything currently re-exported from `src/api/index.ts` needs to keep its current types and call signature. If `ApiClient.fetch` was returning `Promise<any>`, I should keep that loose return type at the boundary even if I tighten internally — narrowing a public return type is technically a breaking change for callers that relied on the looseness.

2. **Idempotency assumptions.** A retry wrapper must not blindly retry POSTs. The simplest first cut is to wrap only the `fetch` call but only retry on networking errors (TypeError) and `5xx`/`429` responses, which are safe to retry for any HTTP method on the assumption the server hasn t partially applied the request. For full safety the call sites would need to pass an explicit `idempotent: true` flag, but that is a larger change and not what the user asked for.

3. **Error shape.** I will introduce `ApiError extends Error` with `status: number` and `path: string` so call sites can pattern-match. I will *not* throw it from the inner fetch; the wrapper catches the raw failure and rethrows as `ApiError` so the public type contract is one error class instead of TypeError vs. Response vs. SyntaxError-from-json.

4. **Existing tests.** Before refactoring I want to see what the test suite currently asserts about error behavior. If a test expects a raw `TypeError` to propagate, I will need to update it. If tests are weak, I will mention it but not block.

My plan, in order: read the current client, locate the call sites with grep, apply a `str_replace` that keeps the public API but routes through `this.retry`, then run the test suite to see what breaks. If the tests fail I will iterate.
Got it. Three methods, one outbound `fetch`. Before I refactor, let me locate the call sites so I can tell whether wrapping the inner call is sufficient.

{"totalMatches":3,"totalFiles":3,"matches":[{"file":"src/App.tsx","lines":[{"lineNo":22,"text":"  const data = await client.fetch(\"/api/items\");"}]},{"file":"src/hooks/useResource.ts","lines":[{"lineNo":18,"text":"    const r = await client.fetch(url);"}]},{"file":"src/services/sync.ts","lines":[{"lineNo":41,"text":"  const next = await client.fetch(`/api/sync/${cursor}`);"}]}]}

Good — three call sites, all just doing `await client.fetch(path)`. Wrapping the inner call keeps them unchanged. Applying the refactor now:

I am going to make two small structural changes in one shot — wrap the inner `fetch` in `this.retry(...)` and throw an `ApiError` on non-2xx responses. The retry helper will live as a private method on `ApiClient` since the user wants the public exports stable. The `ApiError` class will be exported, since callers should be able to type-narrow on it.

function.name: str_replace

content: Refactor applied. Let me run the test suite to make sure I have not broken anything.

{"stdout":"PASS  src/parser.test.ts
PASS  src/store.test.ts
FAIL  src/api/client.test.ts
  ● ApiClient › retries on 5xx
","stderr":"  Expected ApiError, received TypeError
    at Object.<anonymous> (src/api/client.test.ts:42:18)","exitCode":1}
The test expects an `ApiError` but we are throwing a raw `TypeError` from `fetch`. The retry wrapper needs to catch that and rethrow as `ApiError`.
